narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try based on academic and personal factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, LSAT score of 28, Year 1 school decile of 5th, Year 3 decile of 2nd, and plan to attend full-time—and estimated a 45% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed on the first attempt. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2nd, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled a potential dip in later academic performance to the model. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, which is stronger than the dataset average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction toward expecting a bar exam pass. Your Year 1 decile of 5th, close to the average of 5.71, also worked in your favor by suggesting solid early performance, though not enough to fully offset the Year 3 concern. While your LSAT score of 28 is below the typical 36.7 and your full-time status aligns with most applicants, these played lesser roles compared to your decile rankings and GPA. Overall, your situation compares to typical students by having a respectable undergrad record but standing out negatively due to that sharp drop in Year 3 standing, which the model weighed heavily as a red flag for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from an upper-middle-income family—many in similar demographics succeed with stronger late-stage academics. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated transcripts that show upward momentum. You might also explore part-time options or schools with more flexible bar passage criteria while building practical experience like internships. With targeted improvements, especially in those later deciles, you can shift the model's view and open doors to admission. Keep pushing—your solid foundation positions you well for a comeback.
